parsing_res_list.block_content: THEME 1
# Transport of Food and Minerals in Plants

This theme deals with the movement of water containing minerals and food with plants. The exchange of water, gases, minerals and other substances into and out of the cells and also between neighbouring cells, takes place through a system called transportation system. In unicellular organisms (Chlamydomonas) and simple multicellular organisms like Spirogyra, diffusion is the major method of transportation. Diffusion of water across a semipermeable membrane is called osmosis. In complex higher plants because of enormity of size and complex organization, there is an elaborate transportation system and transport occurs through a vascular system of independent channels or conducting tubes (xylem and phloem). In addition to transport, xylem tissue also provides mechanical strength to the plant body. Essential mineral nutrients are also needed for the healthy growth of plants. In the absence or non-availability of the essential elements, the plant shows specific deficiency symptoms.
##### KEY CONCEPTS
Diffusion – definition
Transpiration – definition, importance and factors affecting transpiration.
Structure and function of xylem and phloem in detail
Importance of minerals: macro-nutrients and micro-nutrients; three deficiency diseases caused by lack of these essential nutrients

markdown.text:
THEME 1 # Transport of Food and Minerals in Plants
This theme deals with the movement of water containing minerals and food with plants. The exchange of water, gases, minerals and other substances into and out of the cells and also between neighbouring cells, takes place through a system called transportation system. In unicellular organisms (Chlamydomonas) and simple multicellular organisms like Spirogyra, diffusion is the major method of transportation. Diffusion of water across a semipermeable membrane is called osmosis. In complex higher plants because of enormity of size and complex organization, there is an elaborate transportation system and transport occurs through a vascular system of independent channels or conducting tubes (xylem and phloem). In addition to transport, xylem tissue also provides mechanical strength to the plant body. Essential mineral nutrients are also needed for the healthy growth of plants. In the absence or non-availability of the essential elements, the plant shows specific deficiency symptoms. ##### KEY CONCEPTS Diffusion – definition Osmosis – definition, example semipermeable membrane, root pressure; active transport Transpiration – definition, importance and factors affecting transpiration. Structure and function of xylem and phloem in detail Importance of minerals: macro-nutrients and micro-nutrients; three deficiency diseases caused by lack of these essential nutrients

parsing_res_list.block_content: # Transport of Food and Minerals in Plants
#### Learning Outcomes
Students will be able to :
explain that transport in unicellular and simple multicellular plants takes place by diffusion;
learn about the existence of a transport system inside the body of complex multicellular higher plants;
define and discuss diffusion, osmosis, transpiration, root pressure;
perform experiments and demonstrate the process of osmosis;
infer that the minerals required are either micronutrients or macronutrients depending upon the quantity required by the plants;
relate that the deficiency or lack of essential nutrients leads to specific symptoms and diseases;
define transpiration, interpret its role in xylem transport and know about the factors affecting rate of transpiration;
demonstrate transpiration through simple experiments.
##### Transport System
#### KEY IDEAS
Diffusion is the method of transport of materials in single celled and simple multicellular plants.
In tall plants, diffusion occurs slowly. It cannot move materials quickly enough and in sufficient quantities. So, conducting tissues in plants have developed.
## INTRODUCTION
UP1LE9BNAG

Living organisms need food, water, and oxygen for their survival. The green plants require carbon dioxide, water and sunlight to prepare their food during photosynthesis. For this purpose these substances are needed to be absorbed and then carried (transported) to various plant parts. In addition, waste materials produced in plants need to be removed. In order to carry out all these functions, there is a system of transport in all the organisms including plants.
Before proceeding further, let us understand, what does the word transport mean?

In simple words, transport means to carry people or goods from one place to another by means of vehicles, aircraft, etc. In biology, transport in plants refers to the process of carrying substances like food, water and minerals from one part of the plant to another.
Fig. 1.1 : Transport system in plants
#### SCI-BITS
Xylem is popularly known as wood in mature plants.
## TRANSPORT IN UNICELLULAR AND SIMPLE
## MULTICELLULAR PLANTS
Diffusion is the major process by which transport of materials occurs in single-celled (unicellular) organisms like Chlamydomonas and simple multicellular plants like Spirogyra.
Diffusion is the net movement of molecules (of gas, liquid and solid) from the region of their higher concentration to the region of their lower concentration until they are spread out evenly.
## TRANSPORT IN HIGHER PLANTS (CONDUCTION)

The mechanism of transport is much more elaborate in multicellular higher plants. The process of diffusion cannot meet the requirements of transport in higher plants as it is a slow process. In such plants, there are special channels called xylem and phloem, which comprises conducting tissues or vascular tissues.
Hence, in higher plants, transport of materials like water, minerals and sugar takes place through xylem and phloem. (Fig. 1.1):

Movement of water and minerals takes place from the roots to other plant parts through the stem. Upward movement of water and minerals occurs through channels, called xylem elements, which are present in roots, stem and leaves of plants.
Movement of food material, synthesised in the leaves, occur in soluble form through another channel, called phloem. It transports the food from the leaves to all other parts of the plant's body.
#### STRUCTURE AND FUNCTIONS OF XYLEM AND
##### PHLOEM
Xylem and phloem are the complex, vascular tissues in plant body. While xylem conducts water and minerals, phloem conducts food materials. Let us discuss about these tissues in detail.
##### Xylem
#### (a) Characteristics
Xylem is a complex tissue, as it consists of both parenchymatous (living thin wall cells) and sclerenchymatous (dead thick walled) cells.
Xylem is composed of four elements as follows :

Fig. 1.1 : Transport system in plants
#### SCI-BITS Xylem is popularly known as wood in mature plants. ## TRANSPORT IN UNICELLULAR AND SIMPLE ## MULTICELLULAR PLANTS Diffusion is the major process by which transport of materials occurs in single-celled (unicellular) organisms like Chlamydomonas and simple multicellular plants like Spirogyra. Diffusion is the net movement of molecules (of gas, liquid and solid) from the region of their higher concentration to the region of their lower concentration until they are spread out evenly. ## TRANSPORT IN HIGHER PLANTS (CONDUCTION) The mechanism of transport is much more elaborate in multicellular higher plants. The process of diffusion cannot meet the requirements of transport in higher plants as it is a slow process. In such plants, there are special channels called xylem and phloem, which comprises conducting tissues or vascular tissues. Hence, in higher plants, transport of materials like water, minerals and sugar takes place through xylem and phloem. (Fig. 1.1): Movement of water and minerals takes place from the roots to other plant parts through the stem. Upward movement of water and minerals occurs through channels, called xylem elements, which are present in roots, stem and leaves of plants. Movement of food material, synthesised in the leaves, occur in soluble form through another channel, called phloem. It transports the food from the leaves to all other parts of the plant's body. #### STRUCTURE AND FUNCTIONS OF XYLEM AND ##### PHLOEM Xylem and phloem are the complex, vascular tissues in plant body. While xylem conducts water and minerals, phloem conducts food materials. Let us discuss about these tissues in detail. ##### Xylem #### (a) Characteristics Xylem is a complex tissue, as it consists of both parenchymatous (living thin wall cells) and sclerenchymatous (dead thick walled) cells. Xylem is composed of four elements as follows :

parsing_res_list.block_content: (A) Tracheid
(B) Vessels
Fig. 1.2 : Xylem elements
Fig. 1.3 : Phloem tissue
(i) A tracheid (Fig. 1.2 A) is a non-living, elongated cell with tapering ends. The wall is highly thickened with lignin, except at certain circular spots known as pits.

(ii) A vessel (Fig. 1.2 B) is a cylindrical tube-like structure placed one above the other, end to end. The end walls of vessels dissolve partially or completely, forming a continuous channel. A vessel is also a non-living cell with lignified walls.
Tracheids and vessels are the main conducting elements in the xylem. They both also provide mechanical support to plants.
(iii) Xylem parenchyma (Fig. 1.2C) consists of thick walled living cells. It performs the function of storage.
(iv) Xylem fibres (Fig. 1.2D) are non-living, thick-walled cells which provide mechanical support to plants.
##### (b) Occurrence
Xylem and phloem usually occur together in the roots, stems and leaves of higher plants and form vascular bundles.
##### (c) Functions
The main function of xylem is the unidirectional conduction of water and minerals from the roots to different parts of the plant body.
##### Phloem
Tracheids and xylem fibres provide mechanical support.
Xylem parenchyma helps in the storage of starch and other materials.
##### (a) Characteristics
Phloem is also a complex tissue, consisting of both parenchymatous and sclerenchymatous cells.
Phloem also consists of both living and non-living cells.
Phloem is composed of four elements (Fig. 1.3) as follows :

(i) Sieve tubes are the main conducting elements in the phloem. These are elongated, cylindrical, thin walled cells arranged in vertical rows. The end wall of each sieve tube is perforated, i.e., it has fine pores, like those in a sieve. The perforated end walls are therefore termed as sieve plates. The pores or perforations are called sieve areas. Food materials can easily pass through sieve areas. At maturity, sieve tubes lack the nucleus. Yet, the sieve tubes remain living due to the presence of adjacent companion cells.

Phloem also consists of both living and non-living cells. Phloem is composed of four elements (Fig. 1.3) as follows : (i) Sieve tubes are the main conducting elements in the phloem. These are elongated, cylindrical, thin walled cells arranged in vertical rows. The end wall of each sieve tube is perforated, i.e., it has fine pores, like those in a sieve. The perforated end walls are therefore termed as sieve plates. The pores or perforations are called sieve areas. Food materials can easily pass through sieve areas. At maturity, sieve tubes lack the nucleus. Yet, the sieve tubes remain living due to the presence of adjacent companion cells.

parsing_res_list.block_content: (ii) Companion cells are associated with the sieve tubes. These are smaller cells having dense cytoplasm and prominent nuclei. These cells have a provide direct connections with the sieve tubes. The companion cells help the sieve tubes in the conduction of food material.
(iii) Phloem parenchyma is composed of thin-walled, living parenchymatous cells. It helps in the storage of food.

(iv) Phloem fibres consist of sclerenchymatous elongated, dead and

lignified cells. They provide mechanical support to the plants.
### (b) Occurrence
Phloem is present in all the parts of plant.
#### (c) Functions
The main function of phloem is the bidirectional conduction of food material from the leaves to the other regions of plants and also to storage organs.
Phloem parenchyma helps in the storage of organic matter.
Phloem fibres provide mechanical support to plant.
### INVESTIGATION 1.1
Fig. 1.4
A. Cross section of stem
B. Longitudinal section of stem
To show upward movement of water in a plant shoot.
Take a shoot (a piece of stem with leaves) of balsam plant and dip it in a dilute red coloured dye (either eosin or safranin) in a flask as shown in Fig. 1.4. Leave it for some time.
Fig. 1.5
Then, take sections from different parts of the stem. Examine the sections under the low power of a microscope.
##### What do you observe?
You will observe red colour in the section of stem.

Now, check whether the entire stem tissue is red or only some part of the section is red.

You will find that only certain parts of the stem's section are red (Fig. 1.5). The stained area is xylem through which water is transported along plant's parts.

parsing_res_list.block_content: ### INVESTIGATION 1.2
Fig. 1.6 : Girdling experiment
To show transport of food material in a plant.
This is easily demonstrated by an experiment commonly known as ‘girdling experiment’. Girdling is the process of removal of phloem tissue (bark) from the wood (Fig. 1.6A).
If a stem is girdled, the downward movement of food material gets blocked. Therefore, the trunk portion shows swelling in the girdled area due to the accumulation of food material.
To perform this activity, select a small tree. Gently remove the soft part (bark) of the stem, in a small

area of about 1 to 1.5 inch with the help of a razor blade, as shown in Fig. 1.6 A.

Leave it after girdling for a week. Observe the change in the portion above and below the girdled area. You will observe that the portion above the girdled area will swell due to the accumulation of food materials and the portion below it will not show any swelling.
Fig. 1.7 : Root hair in a seedling
##### Absorption of Water by roots
Roots perform two major functions :
1. Anchor (fix) the plant to the soil.
##### Structure of Roots

Look at the root shown in a seedling (Fig. 1.7). It is a usually cylindrical, non-green underground part of the plant. Just behind the tip of the root, certain unicellular microscopic hairs are present. These are called the root hair.
ZDR52C07Q9
2. Take up (absorb) water and mineral salts from the soil.
Water and mineral salts are absorbed through these root hairs from the soil. Water moves from the soil into the root hair by a process called osmosis.
#### KEY IDEAS
A plant does not have blood or a heart.
Circulation in plants is carried out by a liquid called sap. Movement of sap occurs through xylem and phloem elements.
#### OSMOSIS
Osmosis is the process by which water molecules move through a semipermeable membrane from an area of its higher concentration to an area of its lower concentration.
Osmosis is a special kind of diffusion involving the movement of water molecules.
It occurs when two solutions are separated by a partially permeable membrane (semipermeable membrane).
公

### INVESTIGATION 1.2
Fig. 1.6 : Girdling experiment
To show transport of food material in a plant. This is easily demonstrated by an experiment commonly known as ‘girdling experiment’. Girdling is the process of removal of phloem tissue (bark) from the wood (Fig. 1.6A). If a stem is girdled, the downward movement of food material gets blocked. Therefore, the trunk portion shows swelling in the girdled area due to the accumulation of food material. To perform this activity, select a small tree. Gently remove the soft part (bark) of the stem, in a small area of about 1 to 1.5 inch with the help of a razor blade, as shown in Fig. 1.6 A. Leave it after girdling for a week. Observe the change in the portion above and below the girdled area. You will observe that the portion above the girdled area will swell due to the accumulation of food materials and the portion below it will not show any swelling.
Fig. 1.7 : Root hair in a seedling ##### Absorption of Water by roots Roots perform two major functions : 1. Anchor (fix) the plant to the soil. ##### Structure of Roots
Look at the root shown in a seedling (Fig. 1.7). It is a usually cylindrical, non-green underground part of the plant. Just behind the tip of the root, certain unicellular microscopic hairs are present. These are called the root hair. ZDR52C07Q9 2. Take up (absorb) water and mineral salts from the soil. Water and mineral salts are absorbed through these root hairs from the soil. Water moves from the soil into the root hair by a process called osmosis. #### KEY IDEAS A plant does not have blood or a heart. Circulation in plants is carried out by a liquid called sap. Movement of sap occurs through xylem and phloem elements. #### OSMOSIS Osmosis is the process by which water molecules move through a semipermeable membrane from an area of its higher concentration to an area of its lower concentration. Osmosis is a special kind of diffusion involving the movement of water molecules. It occurs when two solutions are separated by a partially permeable membrane (semipermeable membrane).

parsing_res_list.block_content: ### SCI-BITS
## Partially permeable membrane or semipermeable membrane

The cell wall is freely permeable as it allows almost all the substances to move through it.

A membrane that allows certain substances to pass through it, but not others is called a partially permeable membrane or semipermeable membrane. It is a thin membrane, having small pores.

Generally such membranes allow passage (diffusion) of solvent molecules but do not allow the passage of solute molecules through them. For example, plasma membrane is a semipermeable membrane. Egg membrane and cellophane paper are also semipermeable membranes.
Fig. 1.8 : Process of Osmosis
Fig. 1.9 : The pathway of water across a root and higher up the plant
##### Ascent of Sap
Water moves into plant cells by osmosis in following manner:
The cell membrane of the plant cell is a partially permeable membrane.
The cell sap inside the vacuole is a strong solution which contains high concentration of solutes.
Water passes into the root hair cell by osmosis because soil contains high concentration of water as compared to root hairs.
The concentration of the sap in the vacuole becomes low due to the high concentration of water.
Hence, water passes from one cell to another cell by osmosis due to the concentration gradient in adjacent cells (Fig. 1.8).
The process continues until water reaches the xylem tissue in the roots. From the xylem, water moves upwards from one cell to another against gravity (Fig. 1.9) due to transpiration.
### INVESTIGATION 1.3
A. Raisins in water
B. Grapes in sugar solution
Fig. 1.10
### Experiment to demonstrate osmosis.
Take two bowls.
Take some raisins (dried grapes) and keep them in one bowl (labelled A) containing water.

In the second bowl (labelled B) keep some grapes

in a sugar solution.
Observe them after few hours.

### SCI-BITS ## Partially permeable membrane or semipermeable membrane The cell wall is freely permeable as it allows almost all the substances to move through it. A membrane that allows certain substances to pass through it, but not others is called a partially permeable membrane or semipermeable membrane. It is a thin membrane, having small pores. Generally such membranes allow passage (diffusion) of solvent molecules but do not allow the passage of solute molecules through them. For example, plasma membrane is a semipermeable membrane. Egg membrane and cellophane paper are also semipermeable membranes.
Fig. 1.8 : Process of Osmosis
Fig. 1.9 : The pathway of water across a root and higher up the plant
##### Ascent of Sap Water moves into plant cells by osmosis in following manner: The cell membrane of the plant cell is a partially permeable membrane. The cell sap inside the vacuole is a strong solution which contains high concentration of solutes. Water passes into the root hair cell by osmosis because soil contains high concentration of water as compared to root hairs. The concentration of the sap in the vacuole becomes low due to the high concentration of water. Hence, water passes from one cell to another cell by osmosis due to the concentration gradient in adjacent cells (Fig. 1.8). The process continues until water reaches the xylem tissue in the roots. From the xylem, water moves upwards from one cell to another against gravity (Fig. 1.9) due to transpiration. ### INVESTIGATION 1.3
A. Raisins in water
B. Grapes in sugar solution
Fig. 1.10
### Experiment to demonstrate osmosis. Take two bowls. Take some raisins (dried grapes) and keep them in one bowl (labelled A) containing water. In the second bowl (labelled B) keep some grapes in a sugar solution. Observe them after few hours.

parsing_res_list.block_content: A. Raisins have swollen
B. Grapes have shrunk
Fig. 1.11
### What do you observe?
In bowl A, the raisins have swollen.
In bowl B, the grapes have shrunk.
The raisins contained a concentrated sap and were kept in pure water therefore water molecules from the bowl moved in through the partially permeable membrane of the raisins.
Similarly, as the grapes were kept in a stronger (sugar) solution, water moved out from the grapes through the partially permeable membrane of the grapes.
Fig. 1.12 : Transpiration
#### TRANSPIRATION
Transpiration is the loss of water in the form of water vapour from the leaves to the atmosphere (Fig. 1.12).
The plants absorb much more water through their root systems than required for various life activities like growth and photosynthesis. What happens to the rest of the water?
The excess water is lost by the plants in the vapour form into the surroundings by the process of transpiration. It occurs through stomata present in the leaves by the process of diffusion.

Water absorbed through the roots is moved upwards within the plant body. A small amount of water is used in the process of photosynthesis, and some of it is also used to keep the plant erect and prevent its wilting (drooping).

The remaining water is lost in the form of water vapour into the atmosphere from the leaves. The evaporation of water from leaves produces a suction pull which helps to pull the water upwards from roots to branches. This continuous flow of water from roots to leaves and other aerial parts, through xylem is called transpiration pull.
### Importance of Transpiration
It results in the transport of water and minerals from the soil to the leaves by creating the transpiration pull. In the leaves, water serves as the raw material for photosynthesis.
It produces a cooling effect in plants which helps in preventing the damage of delicate cells due to strong sunlight. It also cools down the surroundings of plants.

The evaporation of water from leaves produces a suction pull which helps to pull the water upwards from roots to branches. This continuous flow of water from roots to leaves and other aerial parts, through xylem is called transpiration pull. ### Importance of Transpiration It results in the transport of water and minerals from the soil to the leaves by creating the transpiration pull. In the leaves, water serves as the raw material for photosynthesis. It produces a cooling effect in plants which helps in preventing the damage of delicate cells due to strong sunlight. It also cools down the surroundings of plants.

parsing_res_list.block_content: ### INVESTIGATION 1.4
To demonstrate transpiration in plants.
What do you need?
Flask containing water
Plant twig
Oil and cotton
##### How to do the test?

Take the flasks half filled with water. Mark clearly the level of water in each flask. Pour small amount of oil on the surface of water to prevent its evaporation. Put plant twig in flask (a). Add the cotton plug at the opening of both the flasks to prevent the loss of water.

Now leave the apparatus for 24 hours. After 24 hours,

note the level of water in both the flasks.

The water level would drop in flask (a) due to the

transpiration through leaves.
#### INVESTIGATION 1.5
To demonstrate that water is given off during transpiration.
##### What do you need?
Small-sized well-watered potted plant
Transparent polythene bag
Thread
##### How to do the test
Take a small-sized well-watered potted plant.
Cover the plant as shown in the figure, with a transparent polythene bag. Tie the bag at the base of the stem.
Leave the plant in sunlight for a few hours, and then observe it. What do you observe?
Drops of water would appear on the inner side of the bag. Water vapour transpired by the plant condenses as water droplets.
##### Factors affecting Transpiration

Intensity of light : Light affects transpiration by influencing the opening and closing of stomata. During the day, stomata are open, and during the night stomata get closed. Stomatal opening increases the water loss from the plant.
Temperature : Higher the temperature, more is the rate of transpiration.

parsing_res_list.block_content: ##### SCI-BITS
The root pressure theory was given in the early eighteen century to explain the rise of water in plants. This force alone, however, cannot explain the rise of water in tall trees.
Fig. 1.15: Active transport
#### KEY IDEAS

Living root cells produce energy that is used to help in the uptake of water.

Wind : Transpiration increases with the velocity of wind.
Humidity: Transpiration is reduced if the atmosphere around the plant or leaf is more humid.
Availability of soil water : When the soil gets dry, the amount of available water also decreases. This leads to reduction in the rate of transpiration.
#### ROOT PRESSURE

Root pressure is the pressure under which water moves from the living cells of a root into the xylem. It is developed in the roots due to the accumulation of water absorbed by them. This root pressure is one of the forces responsible to transport water through the stem into the leaves.
#### ACTIVE TRANSPORT

The movement of molecules across a semipermeable membrane against a concentration gradient using energy is called active transport (Fig. 1.15). The minerals are absorbed by the root hairs from soil through active transport. The root hair cells contain high concentration of minerals as compared to that in soil. Hence, the movement of minerals from soil to root hair cells requires energy.

On the other hand, passive absorption of water from the soil also takes place, which is controlled by transpiration. The force is created by the transpiration pull and the roots simply act as a passive absorbing surface. In passive transport, energy is not involved.
#### MINERALS IN PLANTS
Plants obtain minerals from three sources — atmosphere, water, and soil. Minerals absorbed by plants are returned to the soil by the death and decay of plants and animals.
##### Essential Elements
Though a large number of elements are present in the plant body, only some are important for the healthy growth of plants. These are called essential elements.
In all, 16 elements are found to be essential. Depending upon the quantity of these elements required by the plants, the elements are grouped into two categories:
(i) Macronutrients, which are required in large quantities.
Examples of Macronutrients: Carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, nitrogen, phosphorus, potassium, sulphur, calcium and magnesium.
(ii) Micronutrients, which are required in trace amounts. These are also called trace elements.
Examples of Micronutrients : Chlorine, iron, copper, boron, molybdenum, zinc, manganese, etc.

markdown.text:
##### SCI-BITS The root pressure theory was given in the early eighteen century to explain the rise of water in plants. This force alone, however, cannot explain the rise of water in tall trees.
Fig. 1.15: Active transport
#### KEY IDEAS Living root cells produce energy that is used to help in the uptake of water. Wind : Transpiration increases with the velocity of wind. Humidity: Transpiration is reduced if the atmosphere around the plant or leaf is more humid. Availability of soil water : When the soil gets dry, the amount of available water also decreases. This leads to reduction in the rate of transpiration. #### ROOT PRESSURE Root pressure is the pressure under which water moves from the living cells of a root into the xylem. It is developed in the roots due to the accumulation of water absorbed by them. This root pressure is one of the forces responsible to transport water through the stem into the leaves. #### ACTIVE TRANSPORT The movement of molecules across a semipermeable membrane against a concentration gradient using energy is called active transport (Fig. 1.15). The minerals are absorbed by the root hairs from soil through active transport. The root hair cells contain high concentration of minerals as compared to that in soil. Hence, the movement of minerals from soil to root hair cells requires energy. On the other hand, passive absorption of water from the soil also takes place, which is controlled by transpiration. The force is created by the transpiration pull and the roots simply act as a passive absorbing surface. In passive transport, energy is not involved. #### MINERALS IN PLANTS Plants obtain minerals from three sources — atmosphere, water, and soil. Minerals absorbed by plants are returned to the soil by the death and decay of plants and animals. ##### Essential Elements Though a large number of elements are present in the plant body, only some are important for the healthy growth of plants. These are called essential elements. In all, 16 elements are found to be essential. Depending upon the quantity of these elements required by the plants, the elements are grouped into two categories: (i) Macronutrients, which are required in large quantities. Examples of Macronutrients: Carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, nitrogen, phosphorus, potassium, sulphur, calcium and magnesium. (ii) Micronutrients, which are required in trace amounts. These are also called trace elements. Examples of Micronutrients : Chlorine, iron, copper, boron, molybdenum, zinc, manganese, etc.

parsing_res_list.block_content: #### Importance of Minerals
These are essential for the growth of plants.
These help to provide nutrition to plants.
In the absence of minerals, either the vegetative or reproductive phase cannot be completed.
In case of deficiency, disorders may develop, which can only be corrected by the supply of that mineral.
Table : Deficiency diseases caused due to the lack of essential nutrients

MineralNeeded forDeficiency symptom
1. Macronutrient – NitrogenSynthesis of proteins and nucleic acidPoor growth and old leaves turn yellow (chlorosis).
2. Macronutrient – PotassiumEnzyme activation, water balanceLeaf tips become yellow
3. Macronutrient – MagnesiumA major component of chlorophyllChlorosis (yellowing of leaves).
4. Micronutrient – CopperActivates certain enzymesBluish hue in leaves
5. Micronutrient – MolybdenumNitrogen fixationStunting and leaves fail to develop dark green colour
6. Micronutrient – ZincActivates certain enzymes, auxin synthesisPale leaves, dark spots on edges
#### HIGHLIGHTS IN REVIEW
Transport system helps in moving substances like food, water and minerals from one part of the body to another part.
In unicellular and simple multicellular plants, transport of materials takes place by diffusion.
Higher plants have a transport system comprising of tubes called xylem and phloem.
Xylem carries water and mineral salts from the roots to stem and leaves.
Phloem carries dissolved food from the leaves to all parts of the plant.
Water passes into the root hairs from the soil water by osmosis.
Leaves lose water into the surroundings by transpiration.
Transpiration pull allows water to travel from the roots up the stem to the leaves.
- A number of factors affect transpiration. These include intensity of light, temperature, wind velocity, humidity in the atmosphere and availability of soil water.
Root pressure theory had been put forth to explain the rise of water in plants.
Root pressure is the pressure developed in the roots primarily due to the accumulation of solutes and water in the root xylem.
Movement of molecules across a semipermeable membrane against a concentration gradient using energy is called active transport.

16 essential minerals are required by the plants for their growth and nutrition. In the absence of essential minerals, the vegetative or reproductive phase is affected and disorders develop in case of deficiency.

Root pressure theory had been put forth to explain the rise of water in plants. Root pressure is the pressure developed in the roots primarily due to the accumulation of solutes and water in the root xylem. ## 1. Tick (✓) the correct choice
1. Carrying of substances like food, water and minerals from one part to another part of the body is called (a) circulation (b) transpiration (c) excretion (d) transportation
2. Diffusion is the method of transportation in (a) Chlamydomonas (b) human (c) insects (d) fish
3. The conducting tissues in plants do not include (a) vascular tissue (b) xylem (c) sclerenchyma (d) phloem
4. The root hairs absorb water and minerals from the soil by (a) transpiration (b) ascent of sap (c) root pressure (d) osmosis
5. The cell membrane of plant cell is (a) permeable (b) semipermeable (c) selectively permeable (d) none of these

6. Which one of the following is a macronutrient?

(a) copper (b) boron (c) calcium (d) chlorine

(a) xylem (b) vascular tissues (c) phloem (d) stomata

Prepared food material is carried through

(a) xylem

(b) vascular tissues

(c) phloem

(d) Stomata

9. Water from the soil enters the root xylem by the process

(a) diffusion (b) evaporation (c) osmosis (d) transpiration
10. The loss of water through the stomata of leaves is called (a) transpiration (b) wilting (c) evaporation (d) osmosis
### II. Fill in the blanks

3. Movement of water occurs from a  $ \underline{\text{hippo hnu}} $csolution into a  $ \underline{\text{hippen how}} $solution in osmosis.

2. Xylem and phloem are vascular tissues.
3. 'Girdling experiment' demonstrates food synthesis in plants.

4. Root pressure 5. Active transport

1. Xylem

2. Phloem

1. Sieve tube, tracheid, companion cells, phloem fibres
2. Trachieds, vessels, xylem fibres, companion cells
3. Carbon, magnesium, zinc, sulphur

parsing_res_list.block_content: ### VII. Identify the name of following
1. A method by which transport of materials occurs in unicellular plants.
2. Small pores present on the leaves.

3. Conducting tissue through which food materials are transported within the plant body.

4. The term used to describe yellowing of leaves.
### VIII. Answer the following questions
1. Name the two tissues responsible for transporting food and water in higher plants.
2. How does transport of materials take place in unicellular plants?
3. What are root hair? Mention the function performed by the root hair.
4. How does movement and absorption of water take place through the roots?
5. Name the process by which water loss takes place through the leaves.

7 Differentiate between :

(i) osmosis and transpiration

(ii) micronutrients and macronutrients
8. How does transpiration help in the uptake of water from the soil?
9. List four features outlining the importance of minerals in plant life.
### IX. Case Study Based Questions
##### Read the given information carefully and answer the questions that follow.

The mechanism of transport in multicellular higher plants is much more elaborated. The process of diffusion, which is slow, cannot meet the requirements of transport to all parts in a short period of time. In higher plants, there are special channels called xylem and phloem, which comprises of conducting tissues or vascular tissues. It is through these that the transport of materials takes place.
1. The movement of water in plants is (a) bidirectional (b) unidirectional (c) multidirectional (d) any of these
2. The prepared food by leaves is carried through a channel of tissues called (a) phloem (b) xylem (c) parenchyma (d) collenchyma

3. The movement of food is

(a) unidirectional (b) multidirectional (c) bidirectional (d) any of these

4. The tissues carrying water are also called

(a) pith (b) bark (c) fibres
### X. Assertion-Reason Type Questions

In the questions given below, there are two statements marked as Assertion (A) and Reason (R). Read the statements and choose the correct option.

(a) Both (A) and (R) are true

(c) (A) is true and (R) is false

(b) Both (A) and (R) are false

(d) (A) is false and (R) is true

1. Assertion (A) : Roots fix the plant in the soil.

Reason (R) : Roots absorb water and minerals from soil.

### XI. Art-Integration Based Questions

Art-Integration Based Questions

Take some seeds of Moong (green chickpea) and make them sprout. Now take some sprouts (5 - 6) in a bowl and cover the bowl with a moist tissue paper. After 2 to 3 days observe their roots with the help of a hand lens. You will observe the tiny root hairs on the small roots. Draw the picture of seed along with root hairs.
### XII. Problem Solving Based Questions
Meena observed a tree in a park. Initially it used to appear as shown in picture A but after few weeks, it changed in appearance as shown in picture B.
1. Why did the change occur in the structure of the stem? Make a report or write about it in your notebook.
2. Why did the upper trunk swell?
3. What could be the long-term effect of this change in plants?
### XIII. Competency-Focused Questions
1. Consider the following statements about transport in plants:
P - Diffusion is the main method of transport in unicellular organisms like Chlamydomonas.
Q - Xylem is responsible for the upward movement of food in higher plants.
R - Phloem transports food synthesised in leaves to other parts of the plant.
S - Diffusion is sufficient for transport in all types of plants.

Which of the following options is correct?

(a) P and R (b) Q and S (c) P and S (d) P and Q
2. Choose the incorrectly matched pair.
(a) Tracheid - Non-living, elongated cell with tapering ends
(b) Vessel - Cylindrical tube-like structure with lignified walls
(c) Xylem parenchyma - Non-living cells for storage
(d) Xylem fibres - Non-living, thick-walled cells providing mechanical support
3. Xylem ___ (P) ___ and phloem ___ (Q) ___ in plants.
(a) P - conducts water and minerals, Q - transports food
(b) P - provides mechanical support, Q - stores fats
(c) P - stores starch, Q - conducts water
(d) P - conducts hormones, Q - provides mechanical support
4. Which of the following is not a characteristic of phloem tissue?
(a) Phloem consists of both living and non-living cells.
(b) Sieve tubes are the main conducting elements and lack a nucleus at maturity.
(c) Companion cells help in the conduction of water and minerals.
(d) The end walls of sieve tubes contain sieve plates with fine pores for the passage of food materials.

(a) Only the epidermis of the stem

(b) Only the phloem tissue in the stem

(c) Only the xylem tissue in the stem

(d) The entire stem tissue will be red

7. In the girdling experiment when the phloem tissue is removed from the stem, (a) the upward movement of food material will be blocked, causing swelling above the girdled area. (b) the downward movement of water will be blocked, causing swelling below the girdled area. (c) both upward and downward movements of water and food will be blocked. (d) there will be no change in the plant's behaviour after girdling.

8. During an experiment in her science lab, Meera examined the roots of a plant and noticed tiny hair-like structures on the roots. She learned that these root hairs play an important role in absorbing water. What is the process by which water moves from the soil into the root hairs?

(a) Photosynthesis (b) Diffusion (c) Osmosis (d) Evaporation
9. In the given figures A and B represents:
A.

(a) A- Vessel, B- Tracheid

(b) A- Tracheid, B- Xylem parenchyma

(c) A- Xylem parenchyma, B- Vessel

(d) A- Phloem, B- Xylem
10. Why do sieve tubes lack a nucleus at maturity, and how do they remain functional?

10. Why do sieve tubes lack a ducted at maturity, and how do they

(a) They lack a nucleus due to cellular aging, but remain functional due to their thick cell walls.
(b) They lack a nucleus to reduce metabolic demands, but remain functional due to the metabolic support from companion cells.
(c) They lose their nucleus because it hinders the transport of food materials.
(d) They lack a nucleus to make room for water storage, but companion cells take over their functions.
The sieve plates are characterized by fine pores. What is the primary function of these pores?
(a) To allow water to pass through the sieve tubes.

(a) To allow water to pass through the sieve.

(b) To facilitate the passage of dissolved food materials between sieve tubes.
(c) To provide structural stability to the sieve tubes.
(d) To prevent the entry of pathogens into the plant.
